---
title: "Ch 3 — Pair of Linear Equations in Two Variables"
subtitle: "Two lines, three possible fates: one meeting, infinite meetings, none at all"
lesson-id: "cbse10.pair-linear-equations"
tier: "cbse10"
ncert-class: 10
ncert-chapter: 3
ncert-chapter-title: "Pair of Linear Equations in Two Variables"
board-exam: true
prerequisites: [cbse9.linear-equations-two-vars]
unlocks: [cbse10.coordinate-geometry, cbse11.straight-lines, cbse12.matrices]
applied-domains: [research, ai-ml]
estimated-minutes: 45
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

1. Can you plot a line like $2x + y = 5$ on graph paper, by computing two
   points? See
   [`cbse9.linear-equations-two-vars`](../../tier-cbse-09-class-9/04-linear-equations-two-vars/index.qmd).
2. Can you solve $3x = 12$ for $x$? (Yes? Good.)
3. Can you substitute $y = 4 - x$ into $2x + 3y = 11$ and simplify?
4. Do you know what the **slope** of a line means, even informally?

Three yeses, you are ready.
:::

## Touch

Two paper strips. On each, one straight line is drawn. Slide one over the
other and ask: *where do they touch?* Sometimes one point. Sometimes —
when the strips are parallel — never. Sometimes — when the lines coincide —
everywhere along the strip.

Try the widget. Two lines, two equations:
$$a_1 x + b_1 y = c_1, \qquad a_2 x + b_2 y = c_2.$$

```{ojs}
viewof a1 = Inputs.range([-3, 3], {value: 1, step: 0.5, label: "a₁"})
```

```{ojs}
viewof b1 = Inputs.range([-3, 3], {value: 1, step: 0.5, label: "b₁"})
```

```{ojs}
viewof c1 = Inputs.range([-6, 6], {value: 4, step: 1, label: "c₁"})
```

```{ojs}
viewof a2 = Inputs.range([-3, 3], {value: 2, step: 0.5, label: "a₂"})
```

```{ojs}
viewof b2 = Inputs.range([-3, 3], {value: -1, step: 0.5, label: "b₂"})
```

```{ojs}
viewof c2 = Inputs.range([-6, 6], {value: 1, step: 1, label: "c₂"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const w = 420, h = 280, padX = 30, padY = 30;
  const xMin = -6, xMax = 6, yMin = -6, yMax = 6;
  const xS = d3.scaleLinear().domain([xMin, xMax]).range([padX, w - padX]);
  const yS = d3.scaleLinear().domain([yMin, yMax]).range([h - padY, padY]);
  const svg = d3.create("svg").attr("width", w).attr("height", h)
    .style("background", "#fafafa").style("border", "1px solid #ddd");
  svg.append("line").attr("x1", padX).attr("x2", w - padX)
    .attr("y1", yS(0)).attr("y2", yS(0)).attr("stroke", "#bbb");
  svg.append("line").attr("x1", xS(0)).attr("x2", xS(0))
    .attr("y1", padY).attr("y2", h - padY).attr("stroke", "#bbb");
  function drawLine(a, b, c, color) {
    if (Math.abs(b) > 1e-9) {
      const y1 = (c - a * xMin) / b;
      const y2 = (c - a * xMax) / b;
      svg.append("line").attr("x1", xS(xMin)).attr("y1", yS(y1))
        .attr("x2", xS(xMax)).attr("y2", yS(y2))
        .attr("stroke", color).attr("stroke-width", 2);
    } else if (Math.abs(a) > 1e-9) {
      const x = c / a;
      svg.append("line").attr("x1", xS(x)).attr("y1", padY)
        .attr("x2", xS(x)).attr("y2", h - padY)
        .attr("stroke", color).attr("stroke-width", 2);
    }
  }
  drawLine(a1, b1, c1, "#26a");
  drawLine(a2, b2, c2, "#c33");
  // intersection
  const det = a1 * b2 - a2 * b1;
  let label = "";
  if (Math.abs(det) < 1e-9) {
    // parallel or coincident
    if (Math.abs(a1 * c2 - a2 * c1) < 1e-9 && Math.abs(b1 * c2 - b2 * c1) < 1e-9) {
      label = "Coincident — infinitely many solutions";
    } else {
      label = "Parallel — no solution";
    }
  } else {
    const x = (c1 * b2 - c2 * b1) / det;
    const y = (a1 * c2 - a2 * c1) / det;
    label = `Intersection: (${x.toFixed(2)}, ${y.toFixed(2)})`;
    svg.append("circle").attr("cx", xS(x)).attr("cy", yS(y))
      .attr("r", 5).attr("fill", "#0a7");
  }
  svg.append("text").attr("x", padX).attr("y", h - 6)
    .attr("font-size", 13).text(label);
  return svg.node();
}
```

Set $a_1 = 1, b_1 = 1, c_1 = 4$ and $a_2 = 2, b_2 = 2, c_2 = 8$ — what
happens? Set $a_2 = 2, b_2 = 2, c_2 = 6$ — what happens *now*?

## Wonder

1. When do two distinct lines never meet? Look at their equations — what is
   the same and what is different?
2. When do two equations describe *the same* line?
3. When you saw a unique intersection, was the answer always at "nice"
   integer coordinates? What governs that?
4. If you had three equations in two unknowns, would you expect a solution?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## What you might notice

Same slopes, different intercepts → never meet (no solution). Same slopes,
same intercept → identical line (infinite solutions). Different slopes →
exactly one meeting.
:::

## Discover

### Problem 1 — Solve graphically
Plot $x + y = 4$ and $x - y = 2$ on the same axes. Where do they meet?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
For each line, find two points; draw; mark the intersection.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$x + y = 4$ passes through $(0, 4)$ and $(4, 0)$.
$x - y = 2$ passes through $(0, -2)$ and $(2, 0)$.
They cross at $\mathbf{(3, 1)}$. Verify: $3 + 1 = 4$ ✓ and $3 - 1 = 2$ ✓.
</details>

### Problem 2 — Solve by substitution
Solve $2x + y = 7$, $x - y = 2$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
From the second: $y = x - 2$. Substitute into the first.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$2x + (x - 2) = 7 \Rightarrow 3x = 9 \Rightarrow x = 3$. Then $y = 1$.
**$(3, 1)$.**
</details>

### Problem 3 — Solve by elimination
Solve $3x + 4y = 10$, $2x - 2y = 2$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Multiply the second equation by 2 to make $y$-coefficients add to zero with
the first.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Second × 2: $4x - 4y = 4$. Add to first: $7x = 14$, so $x = 2$. From second:
$2(2) - 2y = 2 \Rightarrow y = 1$. **$(2, 1)$.**
</details>

### Problem 4 — When does no solution exist?
Try to solve $2x + 3y = 7$ and $4x + 6y = 9$. What goes wrong?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Multiply the first by 2 and compare with the second.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
First × 2 gives $4x + 6y = 14$. But the second says $4x + 6y = 9$. The same
expression cannot equal two different values. **No solution.** Notice
$\dfrac{a_1}{a_2} = \dfrac{b_1}{b_2} \ne \dfrac{c_1}{c_2}$.
</details>

### Problem 5 — When are there infinitely many solutions?
Try $x + 2y = 3$ and $2x + 4y = 6$. How many solutions?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
The second is just the first multiplied by 2.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Both equations describe the **same line**. Every point on the line is a
solution — **infinitely many**. Here $\dfrac{a_1}{a_2} = \dfrac{b_1}{b_2} =
\dfrac{c_1}{c_2}$ (all equal $\frac{1}{2}$).
</details>

### Problem 6 — A word problem
The cost of $5$ pencils and $7$ pens together is ₹50. The cost of $7$
pencils and $5$ pens together is ₹46. Find the cost of one pencil and one
pen.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Let $x$ = pencil cost, $y$ = pen cost. Two equations.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
Add and subtract the equations cleverly.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$5x + 7y = 50$ and $7x + 5y = 46$. Adding: $12x + 12y = 96$, so
$x + y = 8$. Subtracting: $-2x + 2y = 4$, so $y - x = 2$. Solve: $y = 5$,
$x = 3$. **Pencil ₹3, pen ₹5.**
</details>

::: {.fun-fact}
The "elimination" trick you just used in P6 — adding/subtracting equations to
knock out a variable — was already routine in **Liu Hui's commentary on the
*Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art*** (China, 3rd century CE), where it
is laid out on a counting-board in essentially the form of modern row
operations. **Carl Friedrich Gauss** rediscovered the same procedure in 1810
while computing the orbit of asteroid Pallas, and we now call it **Gaussian
elimination**. So the move you do for two equations on graph paper is the
*exact* algorithm a modern computer runs — on a $10\,000 \times 10\,000$
matrix — to render every video game shadow you have ever seen.
:::

::: {.key-insight}
Two lines in a plane do exactly one of three things — meet at one point,
coincide, or stay parallel. That geometric "trichotomy" *is* the algebraic
trichotomy of a $2 \times 2$ system: unique solution, infinite solutions,
or no solution. Coefficient ratios are how you read the picture without
drawing it.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

A **pair of linear equations in two variables** is a system
$$a_1 x + b_1 y + c_1 = 0, \qquad a_2 x + b_2 y + c_2 = 0,$$
where the $a_i, b_i, c_i$ are real and at least one of $a_i, b_i$ is nonzero
in each equation.

**Three cases (consistency criterion):**

| Ratio condition | Geometry | Solutions |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| $\dfrac{a_1}{a_2} \ne \dfrac{b_1}{b_2}$ | Lines intersect | Unique solution (consistent) |
| $\dfrac{a_1}{a_2} = \dfrac{b_1}{b_2} = \dfrac{c_1}{c_2}$ | Same line | Infinitely many (dependent) |
| $\dfrac{a_1}{a_2} = \dfrac{b_1}{b_2} \ne \dfrac{c_1}{c_2}$ | Parallel | No solution (inconsistent) |

(Caution: with the $+ c$ form rather than $= c$ form, all three constants
flip sign — but the ratios are the same.)

**Solution methods:** graphical, substitution, elimination. The
*cross-multiplication method* of older NCERT is no longer in the
rationalised syllabus but appears in some reference books.

:::

A verse:

> *Compare the ratios, then declare the fate:*
> *Cross — they meet once. Match all three — they're mate.*
> *Match the slopes but not the third — they pass like ships at night.*
> *Three rules, three cases, never out of sight.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> A boat goes 30 km upstream and 44 km downstream in 10 hours. In 13 hours,
> it can go 40 km upstream and 55 km downstream. Find the speed of the boat
> in still water and the speed of the stream.

Let $u$ be the speed in still water (km/h), $v$ the speed of the stream.
Upstream speed $= u - v$; downstream $= u + v$.

Time = distance / speed:
$$\dfrac{30}{u - v} + \dfrac{44}{u + v} = 10, \qquad
\dfrac{40}{u - v} + \dfrac{55}{u + v} = 13.$$

Let $p = \dfrac{1}{u - v}$, $q = \dfrac{1}{u + v}$. Then:
$30p + 44q = 10, \quad 40p + 55q = 13.$

Multiply: first × 4, second × 3 → $120p + 176q = 40$ and $120p + 165q = 39$.
Subtract: $11q = 1 \Rightarrow q = \frac{1}{11}$, so $u + v = 11$.
Back-substitute: $30p = 10 - 4 = 6 \Rightarrow p = \frac{1}{5}$, so
$u - v = 5$.

Solving: $u = 8, v = 3$. **Boat speed 8 km/h, stream speed 3 km/h.**

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** Solve by substitution: $x + y = 14$, $x - y = 4$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$y = 14 - x$ → $x - (14 - x) = 4 \Rightarrow 2x = 18 \Rightarrow x = 9$,
$y = 5$. **$(9, 5)$.**
</details>

**W2.** Solve by elimination: $2x + 3y = 8$, $4x + 6y = 7$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Multiply first by 2.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$4x + 6y = 16$ vs $4x + 6y = 7$ — contradiction. **No solution** (parallel).
</details>

**W3.** Without solving, decide: do $3x + 2y = 5$ and $9x + 6y = 15$ have a
unique, no, or infinitely many solutions?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Compare $\frac{a_1}{a_2}, \frac{b_1}{b_2}, \frac{c_1}{c_2}$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\frac{3}{9} = \frac{2}{6} = \frac{5}{15} = \frac{1}{3}$. All equal:
**infinitely many solutions** — same line.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** A father is three times as old as his son. Five years later he will
be two and a half times. Find their present ages.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Two equations in $f, s$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$f = 3s$ and $f + 5 = 2.5(s + 5)$. Substitute: $3s + 5 = 2.5s + 12.5
\Rightarrow 0.5s = 7.5 \Rightarrow s = 15$, $f = 45$. **Son 15, father 45.**
</details>

**P2.** Solve $\dfrac{2}{x} + \dfrac{3}{y} = 13$ and $\dfrac{5}{x} -
\dfrac{4}{y} = -2$ (where $x, y \ne 0$).

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Substitute $u = 1/x, v = 1/y$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$2u + 3v = 13$, $5u - 4v = -2$. Multiply first by 4, second by 3:
$8u + 12v = 52$, $15u - 12v = -6$. Add: $23u = 46 \Rightarrow u = 2$.
Then $v = 3$. So $x = 1/2, y = 1/3$.
</details>

**P3.** For what value of $k$ does $kx + 3y = k - 3$ and $12x + ky = k$ have
**no solution**?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Need $\frac{a_1}{a_2} = \frac{b_1}{b_2} \ne \frac{c_1}{c_2}$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\frac{k}{12} = \frac{3}{k}$ gives $k^2 = 36$, so $k = \pm 6$. Check
inequality with constants: $\frac{k - 3}{k}$.
- $k = 6$: ratios are $\frac{1}{2} = \frac{1}{2}$, $\frac{3}{6} = \frac{1}{2}$.
  **All three equal — infinitely many solutions, not "no solution".** Reject.
- $k = -6$: $\frac{-6}{12} = -\frac{1}{2}$, $\frac{3}{-6} = -\frac{1}{2}$,
  $\frac{-9}{-6} = \frac{3}{2} \ne -\frac{1}{2}$. ✓ **$k = -6$.**
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** A two-digit number is such that the product of its digits is 18.
When 63 is subtracted from the number, the digits interchange their places.
Find the number.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Let the digits be $x$ (tens) and $y$ (units). Number $= 10x + y$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
$xy = 18$ and $10x + y - 63 = 10y + x$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Second equation: $9x - 9y = 63 \Rightarrow x - y = 7$. With $xy = 18$ and $x
- y = 7$: $x = 9, y = 2$. Number = $\mathbf{92}$. Check: $92 - 63 = 29$. ✓
</details>

**S2.** A train covers 480 km. If its speed is reduced by 8 km/h, it takes
3 hours longer. Find the original speed.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Let speed = $u$, time = $t$. Then $ut = 480$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
New speed $u - 8$, new time $t + 3$, with $(u - 8)(t + 3) = 480$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$ut = 480$ and $(u - 8)(t + 3) = 480$. Expand: $ut + 3u - 8t - 24 = 480$,
i.e. $3u - 8t = 24$. With $t = 480/u$: $3u - 3840/u = 24$, multiply by $u$:
$3u^2 - 24u - 3840 = 0 \Rightarrow u^2 - 8u - 1280 = 0$. Use quadratic
formula: $u = \frac{8 + \sqrt{64 + 5120}}{2} = \frac{8 + 72}{2} = 40$.
**40 km/h.**
</details>

### Board-Pattern Practice

**Q1 (1 mark — MCQ).** The pair of equations $x = a$ and $y = b$ graphically
represents lines that:
(a) intersect at $(a, b)$  (b) are parallel
(c) are coincident  (d) intersect at $(b, a)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$x = a$ is a vertical line. $y = b$ is a horizontal line.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**(a) intersect at $(a, b)$.**
</details>

**Q2 (1 mark — MCQ).** The pair $2x + 3y = 5$ and $4x + 6y = 11$ has:
(a) unique solution  (b) infinitely many  (c) no solution  (d) two solutions.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\frac{2}{4} = \frac{3}{6} = \frac{1}{2}$, but $\frac{5}{11} \ne \frac{1}{2}$.
**(c) no solution.**
</details>

**Q3 (2 marks).** Solve: $3x + 2y = 11$ and $2x + 3y = 4$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Multiply to align coefficients of one variable.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
First × 3, second × 2: $9x + 6y = 33$, $4x + 6y = 8$. Subtract: $5x = 25
\Rightarrow x = 5$. Then $3(5) + 2y = 11 \Rightarrow y = -2$.
**$(5, -2)$.**
</details>

**Q4 (2 marks).** For what value of $k$ does the system $2x + ky = 1$ and
$3x - 5y = 7$ have a **unique** solution?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\frac{a_1}{a_2} \ne \frac{b_1}{b_2}$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\frac{2}{3} \ne \frac{k}{-5}$, i.e. $k \ne -\frac{10}{3}$. **All real $k$
except $-\frac{10}{3}$.**
</details>

**Q5 (3 marks).** Solve graphically: $x + 2y = 6$ and $2x - y = 2$. Find
the area of the triangle formed by the lines and the y-axis.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Plot. Find intersection. The triangle's vertices are: y-intercept of line
1, y-intercept of line 2, and the intersection point.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Line 1 hits y-axis at $(0, 3)$; Line 2 at $(0, -2)$. Intersection: from
substitution, $y = 2x - 2$, $x + 2(2x - 2) = 6 \Rightarrow 5x = 10
\Rightarrow x = 2, y = 2$. So intersection $(2, 2)$.
Triangle vertices: $(0, 3), (0, -2), (2, 2)$. Base on y-axis = $5$, height
= $2$. **Area $= \frac{1}{2} \cdot 5 \cdot 2 = 5$ sq units.**
</details>

**Q6 (3 marks).** A fraction becomes $\frac{4}{5}$ if 1 is added to both
numerator and denominator. If 5 is subtracted from both, it becomes
$\frac{1}{2}$. Find the fraction.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Let fraction = $\frac{x}{y}$. Two equations.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\frac{x + 1}{y + 1} = \frac{4}{5}$ ⟹ $5x - 4y = -1$.
$\frac{x - 5}{y - 5} = \frac{1}{2}$ ⟹ $2x - y = 5$.
From second: $y = 2x - 5$. Substitute: $5x - 4(2x - 5) = -1 \Rightarrow
-3x = -21 \Rightarrow x = 7, y = 9$. **Fraction = $\frac{7}{9}$.**
</details>

**Q7 (5 marks — long answer).** A man travels 600 km partly by train and
partly by car. If he covers 400 km by train and the rest by car, it takes
him 6 hours 30 minutes. If he travels 200 km by train and the rest by car,
he takes 30 minutes longer. Find the speed of the train and that of the car.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Let train speed $= u$ km/h, car speed $= v$ km/h. Time = distance/speed.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
Substitute $p = 1/u, q = 1/v$ to make the equations linear.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\dfrac{400}{u} + \dfrac{200}{v} = 6.5 \quad \text{(i)}$
$\dfrac{200}{u} + \dfrac{400}{v} = 7 \quad \text{(ii)}$
Let $p = 1/u, q = 1/v$:
$400p + 200q = 6.5$ and $200p + 400q = 7$.
Add: $600p + 600q = 13.5 \Rightarrow p + q = 0.0225$.
Subtract (i − ii): $200p - 200q = -0.5 \Rightarrow p - q = -0.0025$.
So $p = 0.01, q = 0.0125$. Hence $u = 100$, $v = 80$.
**Train: 100 km/h, Car: 80 km/h.**
</details>

::: {.callout-note}
**NCERT Exemplar pointer:** Exemplar 3.3 Q3–Q8 cover word-problems shaped
like Q5 and Q6; 3.4 Q4–Q9 are full long-answer pairs like Q7.

**Previous-year pointer:** CBSE 2019 Set 1, Q22 closely matches Q7's
boat/train shape. CBSE 2023 Standard Q33 mirrors Q5's graphical-area
combination.
:::

### Takeaways

If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

1. **Three solution-cases, one ratio test.** A [system of linear equations](/glossary.qmd#system-of-linear-equations) is consistent-unique, consistent-infinite, or inconsistent — read off from $a_1{:}a_2$, $b_1{:}b_2$, $c_1{:}c_2$.
2. **Pick the method that matches the equations.** [Substitution](/glossary.qmd#substitution-method) when one variable is already isolated; [elimination](/glossary.qmd#elimination-method) when coefficients align nicely; graphing only when asked.
3. **Word problems are translation problems.** Name the unknowns, write *one [linear equation](/glossary.qmd#linear-equation) per English sentence*, then solve mechanically.

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"$\frac{a_1}{a_2} = \frac{b_1}{b_2}$ means infinite solutions."** Only
   if **also** $\frac{c_1}{c_2}$ matches. If the constant ratio breaks
   away, you get **no solution** (parallel), not infinite.
2. **"Substitute $1/x = u$ only works if $x \ne 0$."** True. Always check
   that the final answer doesn't make any denominator zero.
3. **"Graphical solution is approximate, so it doesn't count."** In CBSE
   3-mark questions like Q5, the solution must read off **exactly** at the
   given graph paper scale. Always pick scales that make the intersection
   land on a grid point.
:::

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you state the three cases (unique / infinite / none) and tie each
   to its ratio condition, without notes?
2. Did Q7 fall out cleanly with the substitution $p = 1/u, q = 1/v$?
3. Can you recognise when a word problem hides a 2×2 linear system?

- **3 yes:** move on.
- **2 yes:** redo P3 and Q5. Then move on.
- **0–1 yes:** the ratio criterion is doing more work than you realise.
  Re-read *Name* and re-attempt Discover Problems 4–5.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- [`cbse9.linear-equations-two-vars`](../../tier-cbse-09-class-9/04-linear-equations-two-vars/index.qmd) —
  one linear equation in two variables; lines on the plane.

### What this unlocks

- [`cbse10.coordinate-geometry`](../07-coordinate-geometry/index.qmd) —
  lines as geometric objects with distance and division formulas.
- [`cbse11.straight-lines`](../../tier-cbse-11-class-11/09-straight-lines/index.qmd) —
  slope-intercept and point-slope forms.
- [`cbse12.matrices`](../../tier-cbse-12-class-12/03a-matrices-intro/index.qmd) —
  the same systems generalised to $n$ unknowns.

### NCERT chapter alignment

- **Class 10, Chapter 3 — Pair of Linear Equations in Two Variables**
  (NCERT 2023 rationalised syllabus). Covers Sections 3.1–3.4: graphical
  method, substitution, elimination. (Cross-multiplication and 3.6
  reducible-to-linear were trimmed in 2023, though P2 / Q7-style
  $1/x, 1/y$ problems remain board-frequent.)
- **Sister synthesis lesson:**
  [`t2.systems-linear-equations`](../../tier-2-prealgebra/06-systems-linear-equations/index.qmd) —
  the synthesis lens, including row-reduction in matrix shape.
- **Read alongside the NCERT textbook**.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI/ML.** Linear regression — the simplest model in machine learning —
solves a vastly larger version of this same system: thousands of
equations, hundreds of unknowns, with no exact intersection (so we settle
for "closest possible"). Every bit of theory you build here scales up.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Research.** Linear systems are the engine of numerical analysis, finite
element methods, and signal processing. The substitution and elimination
methods you just used are the textbook ancestors of Gaussian elimination
— the workhorse algorithm of scientific computing.
:::

---

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

- **Touchstones:** 3Blue1Brown (lines as geometric objects); Russian
  school (problem-first *Discover*); Bhāskara (compact *Name* table).
- **Source notes:** Q7 follows the standard NCERT train-and-car pattern.
  The ratio-criterion table is the standard NCERT 3.2 framing.
:::
